claims.text: On the night of February 20, 1954, President Eisenhower vanished for an entire evening during his Palm Springs vacation; the Associated Press briefly reported him dead, the White House claimed an emergency dental visit, but reporters could find no dentist, no office, and no record of the appointment.

evidence.quote:

room. He wasn't on the golf course. The
Secret Service searched everywhere. He
was just gone. The Associated Press
reported President Eisenhower died
tonight of a heart attack in Palm
Sprin

asking questions. They couldn't find the
dentist or his office and no record of
the visit.
That's because the story was a lie.
Eisenhower wasn't at the dentist. He wa

claims.rationale: It is well-attested that Eisenhower's whereabouts the evening of February 20, 1954 were briefly mishandled by the press: the AP did file a short-lived bulletin of his death, and the dental-visit cover story did circulate. Modern researchers have located records identifying Dr. Francis Purcell as the dentist visited; the host's claim that 'there's no record' is contested by the Eisenhower Library's own materials, but the underlying confused-disappearance episode is real.

claims.text: Eisenhower's actual destination that night was Edwards Air Force Base for a heavily guarded meeting whose participants — high-ranking generals and top scientists — would later become known by the codename Majestic 12.

hairy problem.

hear.

claims.rationale: The MJ-12 documents that supposedly establish a secret committee meeting at Edwards or Holloman in 1954 originated as anonymous mailings to UFO researchers in 1984 and were declared fabrications by the FBI. There is no credible documentary evidence Eisenhower was at Edwards that night for an extraterrestrial meeting; this is a foundational claim of the Greada/Holloman alien-treaty mythology, not a historical fact.

claims.text: At Edwards two seamless silver disc-shaped craft landed silently and disgorged tall, blonde, telepathic 'Nordic' aliens who offered humanity unlimited clean energy, the end of disease, 300-year lifespans, and a golden age — in exchange for the dismantling of all nuclear weapons.

turning Earth into a paradise. All they
wanted was all nucle

claims.rationale: This is the central narrative element of the Greada Treaty mythology. The 'Nordic' aliens trope is a stable feature of UFO contactee literature from the 1950s (George Adamski, etc.) but has zero independent corroboration. No physical evidence, contemporaneous documentation, or non-anonymous witness ever supported the existence of such a meeting or such beings. The story is well-attested as a story; it has no factual support.

claims.text: The Nordics warned Eisenhower that a parasitic alien race was already on Earth and would offer Eisenhower advanced weapons and technology — including anti-gravity propulsion — without requiring nuclear disarmament, but their gifts would be a trap.

from them. This other race would also
offer tec

claims.rationale: Same evidentiary status as the Nordic offer: this is internal narrative of the Greada myth with no factual support. The 'parasitic' framing of the Grays is one of several variants of the modern alien-treaty mythos popularized by William Cooper.

claims.text: Writer-researcher Gerald Light, in a 1954 letter, claimed to have witnessed multiple distinct alien spacecraft being studied by the Air Force at Edwards alongside extraterrestrials called the Etherians, in the company of credentialed civilians (Truman's economic adviser Dr. Edwin Nourse, Hearst's Franklin Allen, and Cardinal McIntyre).

researcher, wrote about what he saw at
Edward's Air Force Base. Extreme
security measures were in place.
We were finally allowed to enter the
restricted section after about 6 hours
in which we

Edwin Norris had been Truman's chief
economic adviser. Franklin Allen worked
for the Hurst newspaper empire. Cardinal

Church.
These weren't UFO enthusiasts or
conspiracy theorists. They

claims.rationale: Gerald Light did write the famous April 16, 1954 letter to Meade Layne of the Borderland Sciences Research Associates describing such a meeting. The named individuals are real people (though Edwin Nourse, not 'Norris'/'Nurse' — host's transliteration is slightly off). However, none of the named civilians publicly corroborated the meeting; Light was an established occultist whose claims are uncorroborated. The letter exists; the meeting it describes is unverified.

claims.text: A second alien race — the Grays, short with large bald heads and black eyes — arrived shortly after and offered Eisenhower a century of technological supremacy (particle beams, zero-point energy, stealth, anti-gravity propulsion) in exchange for permission to operate underground bases on Earth and conduct ostensibly limited, harmless human abductions with full disclosure to the U.S. government.

claims.rationale: Core element of the Greada Treaty conspiracy mythology, popularized by William Cooper's 1989 lectures and 'Behold a Pale Horse'. No physical evidence, no leaked authentic documents, no contemporaneous testimony from named officials. Several technologies the Grays supposedly delivered (transistors, fiber optics, etc.) have well-documented mundane invention histories that pre-date or run independently of any '1954 treaty'.

claims.text: Eisenhower chose the Grays' offer of power over the Nordics' offer of peace, and a treaty was sealed telepathically; in the years following, the U.S. obtained underground tunneling technology and built the seven-level Dulce facility in New Mexico under joint human-Gray operation.

claims.rationale: The Dulce-base mythology is a classic of UFO/conspiracy literature, with no aerial photography, satellite imagery, geological survey, or whistleblower documentation that has stood up to scrutiny. The town of Dulce, NM is real; the seven-level alien base under it is uncorroborated.

claims.text: Geologist-engineer Phil Schneider, building the Dulce base in 1979, broke through into a vast Gray nesting cavern containing thousands of incubation tanks with human body parts, triggering a firefight in which 66 humans died and only three survived.

claims.rationale: Phil Schneider was a real lecturer on the UFO/conspiracy circuit in the early 1990s and made these exact claims in his talks. However, his alleged credentials (security clearance, military structural-engineering background) have never been independently verified, and military records do not support his Dulce-firefight account. The hand-mutilation he displayed was real but its origin is disputed by his own family. Story is real; underlying events almost certainly fabricated.

claims.text: Former naval intelligence officer William Cooper publicly revealed the alien treaty, claimed first-hand knowledge of Project Sigma's 1953 detection of large incoming spacecraft, and described a witnessed February-1955 Eisenhower meeting with aliens at Holloman Air Force Base.

claims.rationale: Cooper was a real Navy veteran (radio operator, not intelligence officer — his self-described role is exaggerated) who became a major UFO/conspiracy lecturer in the late 80s and 90s. He published 'Behold a Pale Horse'. The claims he made about treaty access, Project Sigma, and the Holloman meeting are unverified; his own later writings became increasingly anti-government and apocalyptic, and his security access was demonstrably overstated.

claims.text: Both Phil Schneider and William Cooper publicly predicted their own deaths and were subsequently killed under suspicious circumstances — Cooper in a 2001 shootout with police serving a tax-evasion warrant, Schneider in 1996 found strangled with surgical tubing and ruled a suicide.

evidence.quote:

that their lives were in danger. Both
said they were being threatened. Both

murdered."

suicide. There's a link below to the

claims.rationale: Cooper was killed in November 2001 in a shootout with Apache County deputies serving an arrest warrant — that much is documented. Schneider died in January 1996; he was found dead with rubber tubing around his neck. Both men had publicly said they expected to be killed. Both deaths are factually accurate as the host states. Whether they were 'killed for what they knew' vs. paranoid self-fulfilling prophecies (Cooper) or suicide (Schneider's official ruling) is interpretive.

claims.text: On June 28, 2001 — six weeks before 9/11 — William Cooper publicly predicted on his radio show that there would be a major false-flag terrorist attack on US soil that would be blamed on Osama bin Laden, a name very few people would have recognized at the time.

evidence.quote:
June 28th, 2001 on his
radio show, Cooper said there would be a
major terrorist attack on US soil. He
said this would be a false flag
operation and Osama bin Laden would be
blamed. Now, this time, very few people
ever he

claims.rationale: Cooper's June 28, 2001 'Hour of the Time' radio broadcast did discuss expected false-flag terrorism that would be blamed on bin Laden — recordings exist. However, bin Laden was hardly an obscure figure: he was on the FBI's Ten Most Wanted list since 1999, had taken responsibility for the 1998 embassy bombings and the 2000 Cole bombing, and was widely covered in news media. The 'very few people had heard of him' framing overstates the case. Prediction itself is real; uniqueness/foreknowledge is overstated.

claims.text: Eisenhower's 1961 farewell address — explicitly warning of the unwarranted influence of the military-industrial complex and the rise of unelected defense-contractor power — was a final, possibly confessional warning by a president who knew firsthand of the secret bargain he had made.

evidence.quote:
farewell address to the nation in 1961,
his final warning wasn't about the
Soviets. It was about a threat from
within. He warned of the growing
unchecked power of a military industrial
complex, a shadow government of
une

claims.rationale: The 1961 farewell address with the MIC warning is real, important, and often cited; the speech text is in the National Archives. Treating it as a 'confession' about an alien treaty is a leap unsupported by anything in the speech itself, which is a coherent civic-republican warning about defense-spending political capture. The host couches this as speculation ('was it a confession?'), so it's appropriately positioned as opinion/speculation rather than asserted as fact.

claims.text: Whether or not the Greada Treaty literally happened, the story's verdict about human nature is correct: offered a choice between enlightenment-through-disarmament and power-through-weapons, humanity would choose power every time, because trusting an unverifiable stranger to disarm first while you remain armed is irrational.

evidence.quote:

practical.

claims.rationale: This is the host's normative verdict and explicit closing thesis. As an opinion claim, no truth value applies. The framing maps onto well-known game-theoretic results in arms-control literature: unilateral disarmament under uncertainty about the other party's compliance is rarely chosen by states. The host's intuition matches a substantial international-relations consensus.
